observations.claim: Bob launched an action covering the entire blueprint, including gathering common materials and placing both common and special blocks.
Andy later launched an action to place all inventory-held common blocks across the same blueprint coordinates, overlapping Bob's common-block scope.
The two active scopes can compete for the same common-material placements; a clean split by block type would preserve their complementary work while removing duplication.
Andy's only recorded attempt to open coordination failed because the supplied target was not recognized as a bot.
Some construction progress occurred, with three correct target placements recorded, so the intervention should clarify remaining scope rather than undo work.

agent_assessments.summary: Andy has an actionable common-material placement scope, but its boundary overlaps Bob's whole-blueprint action and later workload attribution is sparse.
Bob claimed the whole build plus material acquisition rather than a distinct special-block role; actual completed workload cannot be established from the low-attribution window.

manager_advisory.message: Split remaining work by block type: Bob should place only gold, quartz, quartz pillars, and glowstone; Andy should handle stone, bricks, and polished variants. Have both confirm the boundary before continuing.

limitations: The window is nonterminal and covers only the first 90 seconds.
Action commands establish intended scope, not completion or sustained execution.
Only 2 of 27 target changes were confidently agent-attributed (7.4% coverage), so responsibility for most world changes is unknown.
No inventory net changes were recorded, limiting conclusions about material transfer or consumption.

observations.claim: Andy performed substantial masonry work, consuming 34 polished_andesite, 20 polished_diorite, 21 stone, and 39 stone_bricks during the window.
Bob performed the complementary upper-material work and still held 9 gold_block, 7 quartz_block, and 2 quartz_pillar after consuming gold, quartz, and glowstone.
Bob's active scope was to finish all remaining fixes, including both gathering masonry and placing his inventory gold, quartz, and quartz pillars.
Andy was subsequently assigned all remaining fixes too, including seeking gold, quartz, and quartz pillars despite Bob already holding those materials.
The current assignments unnecessarily overlap and no longer match the useful inventory split: Andy can finish the six listed masonry fixes while Bob supplies the remaining upper-tier blocks.

agent_assessments.summary: Andy completed substantial masonry work, but the latest action expands into sourcing scarce upper-tier materials already held by Bob, creating avoidable overlap.
Bob contributed complementary gold, quartz, and glowstone work and retains the needed upper-tier inventory, but his finish-all scope overlaps Andy's latest assignment.

manager_advisory.message: Split the remaining fixes: have Andy place only the six listed masonry blocks, and Bob place the remaining gold, quartz, and quartz-pillar blocks from his inventory. Compare one current blueprint list first to avoid duplicate changes.

limitations: Only 31.9% of target changes have confident agent attribution, so unattributed placements cannot be assigned.
Andy's long build command has an unclear outcome, and command text alone does not prove completion.
The packet ends at 180 seconds, so it cannot show whether the overlapping assignments later self-resolved.
No direct transfer or coordination message between Andy and Bob is recorded in this window.

observations.claim: Bob completed most confidently attributed work in this window: eight correct target placements versus Andy's one.
At cutoff, only stone_bricks at -344 65 252 and stone at -344 66 252 remained, while Andy's latest inventory contained one of each.
Andy had explicitly claimed both remaining placements by 229.574, but neither was complete at Bob's 269.206 blueprint check; Andy had no recorded activity for the final 40.426 seconds.
The remaining work matches Andy's resources and claimed role, yet Bob expanded into gathering basic materials after carrying the gold/quartz phase, creating an actionable workload and role mismatch.

agent_assessments.summary: Andy retains the exact two blocks still needed and had claimed their placement, but recorded no task activity during the last 40 seconds.
Bob performed most attributed placements, consumed the specialty materials, and then took on gathering basic blocks that Andy already holds.

manager_advisory.message: Have Andy immediately place his stone_bricks at -344 65 252 and stone at -344 66 252; Bob should pause material gathering and verify completion afterward.

limitations: Only 9 of 22 correct target changes were confidently attributed, so the exact per-agent placement split is incomplete.
Command outcomes for both newAction calls are marked unclear; their text proves intended scope, not execution.
The packet ends before confirming whether Andy acts on the two remaining placements.

observations.claim: The structure reached a score of 100.0 at the terminal cutoff, with the final increase recorded 0.251 seconds before cutoff.
Two correct target placements occurred in the window, matching the score increase, though neither placement was confidently attributed to an executor.
Bob identified the final two required coordinates and issued a focused action to obtain and place exactly one stone_bricks and one stone.
Andy and Bob had overlapping finishing claims, but completion at the terminal cutoff leaves no unresolved duplicate scope, blockage, or low-regret work to rebalance now.

agent_assessments.summary: Andy had an earlier comprehensive finishing assignment and retained the exact two basic materials, but had no recorded activity in this short final window; placement attribution is unavailable.
Bob continued the finishing phase, checked his depleted inventory, then narrowed work to the final two blocks before the structure reached full score.

limitations: Attribution coverage for both correct placements is 0%, so responsibility cannot be assigned to Andy or Bob.
The packet covers only the terminal 24.772-second window and contains no evidence after cutoff.
Claims in newAction commands establish intended scope, not execution or completion by the claiming agent.
